title: A small thing I learned from my parents
summary: Narrate the moment when one of your parents taught you something small but lasting.
topic: A small thing I learned from my parents
prompt: Write 150–250 words about one small thing you learned from a parent. Describe the exact moment they taught you. Use past simple and 'when' to mark the moment.
hintStarters: My father / mother / grandmother showed me…
I still do this today because…

hintVocab.meaningVi: đến nay tôi vẫn làm vậy

sampleText: I was about ten when my father showed me how to save money. We were sitting at the kitchen table in our small flat in Cairo. He had three white envelopes in front of him. He did not say a long speech. He just put them on the table and looked at me. On the first envelope, he wrote 'needs'. On the second, he wrote 'wants'. On the third, he wrote 'later'. Then he gave me five small coins, all the same. He said, "Put two coins in 'needs', one in 'wants', and two in 'later'. Every time you have money, do it again." That was all. He drank his tea and went back to reading the newspaper. I thought it was a strange game, but I did it anyway. After one year, the 'later' envelope was the heaviest. When my school trip came, I had enough money for the bus and for one big sandwich. I felt very proud, but I felt something bigger too. I felt that I could plan for myself. My father did not say much that day. He used a soft voice and three envelopes. But he gave me a habit that still helps me when I am thirty. The lesson was simple. The patience was the hard part.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past simple to narrate a teaching moment
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'My father showed me', 'he wrote', 'he gave me' — past simple kể trình tự việc đã xảy ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'My father showed me', 'he wrote', 'he gave me' — past simple narrates the sequence of finished actions.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Superlative 'the heaviest'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The 'later' envelope was the heaviest.' — 'Heavy' → 'heaviest', dùng cùng 'the'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The "later" envelope was the heaviest.' Short adjective + -est with 'the'.

sampleTranslationVi: Tôi khoảng mười tuổi khi cha chỉ cho tôi cách tiết kiệm tiền. Chúng tôi đang ngồi ở bàn bếp trong căn hộ nhỏ ở Cairo. Trước mặt cha có ba chiếc phong bì trắng. Cha không nói bài giảng dài. Ông chỉ đặt chúng trên bàn và nhìn tôi. Trên phong bì thứ nhất, cha viết 'nhu cầu'. Phong bì thứ hai, ông viết 'mong muốn'. Phong bì thứ ba, ông viết 'sau này'. Sau đó cha đưa tôi năm đồng xu nhỏ, đều bằng nhau. Cha nói: "Bỏ hai đồng vào 'nhu cầu', một vào 'mong muốn', và hai vào 'sau này'. Mỗi lần con có tiền, làm lại như vậy." Vậy thôi. Cha uống trà và quay lại đọc báo. Tôi nghĩ đó là trò chơi kỳ lạ, nhưng vẫn làm theo. Sau một năm, phong bì 'sau này' là nặng nhất. Khi chuyến đi trường đến, tôi đủ tiền đi xe buýt và mua một chiếc bánh sandwich lớn. Tôi rất tự hào, nhưng còn cảm thấy điều gì lớn hơn. Tôi thấy mình có thể tự lên kế hoạch cho bản thân. Cha tôi không nói nhiều hôm đó. Ông dùng giọng nhẹ nhàng và ba chiếc phong bì. Nhưng cha cho tôi một thói quen vẫn giúp tôi khi đã ba mươi tuổi. Bài học đơn giản. Sự kiên nhẫn mới là phần khó.

options: I was about ten when my father showed me how to save money.
I am about ten when my father shows me how to save money.
I was about ten when my father has shown me how to save money.
When I about ten, my father showed me how to save money.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past simple in both clauses for a finished memory.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past simple of 'show' correctly?
options: He showed me three envelopes.
He shown me three envelopes.
He shows me three envelopes.
He has show me three envelopes.
The 'later' envelope was the heaviest.
The 'later' envelope was the heavyer.
The 'later' envelope was the most heavy.
The 'later' envelope was the most heaviest.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural reported instruction.
options: He said, "Put two coins in 'needs'."
He said, "Putting two coins in 'needs'."
He said, "You putted two coins in 'needs'."
He said, "You will to put two coins in 'needs'."
mcQuestions.explanation: An imperative ('Put') is the natural form for a direct instruction.

options: I thought it was a strange game, but I did it anyway.
I thought it was a strange game, so I did it anyway.
I thought it was a strange game, because I did it anyway.
I thought it was a strange game, while I did it anyway.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'But' shows contrast between thinking it strange and doing it.

options: I had enough money for the bus.
I had enough of money for the bus.
I had money enough the bus.
I had enough money to the bus.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Enough money' (adjective before noun) and 'for + noun' for purpose.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence is in correct past simple?
options: He drank his tea and went back to reading the newspaper.
He drunk his tea and gone back to reading the newspaper.
He drinks his tea and goes back to reading the newspaper.
He has drank his tea and has gone back to reading the newspaper.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Drink → drank' and 'go → went' are correct past simple.
mcQuestions.hint: Two irregular past forms: drink → drank, go → went.

options: He used a soft voice and three envelopes.
He used softly voice and three envelopes.
He using a soft voice and three envelopes.
He has used soft voice and three envelopes.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past simple + adjective 'soft' before the noun 'voice'.

options: He gave me a habit that still helps me when I am thirty.
He gave me a habit that helps still me when I am thirty.
He gave me a habit that helps me still when I am thirty.
He gave me a habit that is still helping me since when I am thirty.
The lesson was simple. The patience was the hard part.
In conclusion, parents teach us important lessons.
Throughout history, families have passed on wisdom.
It is important to note that saving money is a virtue.
mcQuestions.explanation: A short, specific contrast lands harder than a generic line.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is the smallest lesson from your parents the one you remember the most? Why do you think small lessons stay longer than big speeches?
